Decentralized digital currency without i.summary: Bitcoin is the primary example of a decentralized digital currency designed to let people transfer value directly without a central bank or payment intermediary. Its original white paper describes it as a peer-to-peer electronic cash system, and official Bitcoin materials describe the network as user-operated, decentralized, and not controlled by a central authority. Independent reference sources and central-bank explainers likewise note that Bitcoin is maintained by a distributed network of computers rather than issued by a single institution.
keyPoints: Satoshi Nakamoto’s 2008 white paper introduced Bitcoin as a "peer-to-peer electronic cash system," establishing the core idea of direct online payments without financial intermediaries.
Bitcoin.org describes Bitcoin as the first decentralized peer-to-peer payment network, run by its users with no central authority or intermediary.
The European Central Bank states that bitcoin is not issued by a central public authority and is created and tracked by a network of computers using mathematical rules.
Bitcoin transactions are recorded on a distributed blockchain ledger replicated across network nodes, helping prevent double-spending without relying on a bank or central operator.

Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.canonical: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.summary: "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System" is Satoshi Nakamoto’s original Bitcoin whitepaper, dated October 31, 2008. The paper proposes a peer-to-peer electronic payment system that avoids trusted financial intermediaries by combining digital signatures, a proof-of-work chain, and network consensus to prevent double-spending. It describes the core mechanics of Bitcoin, including timestamping transactions into blocks, selecting the longest proof-of-work chain as the valid history, and using Merkle trees and simplified payment verification to support efficiency and lightweight verification.
keyPoints: The whitepaper was published under the name Satoshi Nakamoto on October 31, 2008, and is presented by bitcoin.org as the paper that first introduced Bitcoin.
Its abstract says Bitcoin enables online payments to be sent directly from one party to another without going through a financial institution, framing the system as peer-to-peer electronic cash.

The paper’s central technical contribution is solving double-spending through a peer-to-peer network that timestamps transactions into a hash-based proof-of-work chain; the longest chain is treated as the valid record.
The whitepaper also specifies supporting design elements such as Merkle trees for compact block verification and simplified payment verification (SPV) for lightweight clients.

Bitcoin creation by Satoshi Nakamoto in.canonical: Bitcoin creation by Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008
Bitcoin creation by Satoshi Nakamoto in.summary: Search results confirm that Satoshi Nakamoto introduced Bitcoin in 2008 by publishing the white paper "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System" on October 31, 2008. The live Bitcoin network itself began shortly afterward in early 2009, when Nakamoto mined the genesis block on January 3, 2009, so the most precise formulation is that Bitcoin was proposed in 2008 and launched in 2009.
keyPoints: Multiple sources state that Satoshi Nakamoto published the Bitcoin white paper on October 31, 2008, introducing the design for a decentralized peer-to-peer electronic cash system.
Bitcoin.org was registered in 2008, before the public release of the white paper, supporting the timeline of Bitcoin's development in 2008.
The Bitcoin network did not go live in 2008; sources identify January 3, 2009 as the date Nakamoto created the genesis block, commonly treated as Bitcoin's launch.
Satoshi Nakamoto remains a pseudonym, and later court reporting cited in search results notes that claims by Craig Wright to be Bitcoin's creator were rejected in 2024.

Satoshi Nakamoto.summary: Satoshi Nakamoto is the pseudonym used by the unknown creator or creators of Bitcoin. Search results consistently describe Nakamoto as an anonymous person or possibly a group who authored the 2008 Bitcoin white paper, launched the Bitcoin software in 2009, and has never been definitively identified.
keyPoints: The Bitcoin white paper, "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System," was published in 2008 under the name Satoshi Nakamoto.
Major reference sources describe Satoshi Nakamoto as a pseudonym for an unknown person or group rather than a confirmed individual.
Nakamoto created and released Bitcoin’s original reference implementation and was active in Bitcoin’s early development before withdrawing from public communication.
A UK High Court ruling in March 2024 found that Craig Wright was not Satoshi Nakamoto, rejecting one of the most prominent identity claims.

Bitcoin vs Visa transaction throughput.summary: Search results support the common comparison that Bitcoin’s base-layer blockchain handles roughly 3.3-7 transactions per second, while Visa’s network handles far more. Official Visa materials now state VisaNet can process over 65,000 transaction messages per second, though older Visa materials cited more than 24,000. So the oft-repeated 'Bitcoin 7 TPS vs Visa 24,000 TPS' comparison is directionally correct but uses an older Visa figure.
keyPoints: Bitcoin base-layer throughput is commonly estimated at about 3.3-7 TPS, based on its block size constraints and roughly 10-minute block interval.
Visa’s own recent materials say VisaNet can process over 65,000 transaction messages per second, which is substantially higher than the older 24,000 TPS figure.
A current Visa page comparing blockchains says Bitcoin manages around 7 TPS, aligning with the common estimate used in scalability discussions.
The comparison is not perfectly apples-to-apples: Bitcoin TPS refers to on-chain blockchain transactions, while Visa’s number refers to centralized network transaction message processing capacity.

Bitcoin block reward halving schedule.summary: Bitcoin’s block reward schedule is hard-coded in the protocol: it began at 50 BTC per block when the network launched in 2009 and is reduced by 50% every 210,000 blocks, which works out to roughly every four years. Verified halving steps are 50 BTC at launch, then 25 BTC in November 2012, 12.5 BTC in July 2016, 6.25 BTC in May 2020, and 3.125 BTC after the most recent halving on April 20, 2024.
keyPoints: Bitcoin halves the miner subsidy every 210,000 blocks, not on a fixed calendar date, so the event is only approximately every four years.
The reward history is: 50 BTC (2009 launch), 25 BTC (2012), 12.5 BTC (2016), 6.25 BTC (2020), and 3.125 BTC (2024).
The 2024 halving occurred at block 840,000 on April 20, 2024, reducing new issuance from 6.25 BTC to 3.125 BTC per block.
This declining issuance schedule is tied to Bitcoin’s capped supply of 21 million coins; sources commonly project the next halving in 2028, when the reward would fall to 1.5625 BTC.

Block mining reward process.summary: In Bitcoin’s proof-of-work system, miners compete to find a block header hash below the network’s current target. The miner who first finds a valid hash can broadcast the candidate block; if other nodes verify it, that block is appended to the chain. The winning block includes a special first transaction, called the coinbase transaction, which creates the allowed block subsidy (newly minted bitcoin) and also assigns the block’s transaction fees to the miner.
keyPoints: Bitcoin mining is a proof-of-work race: miners repeatedly hash a candidate block header until one finds a hash below the difficulty target, and that valid block can then be accepted by the network.
The reward is not just newly minted bitcoin; it consists of the block subsidy plus the transaction fees from all transactions included in that block.
The miner claims the reward through the block’s first transaction, the coinbase transaction, which is a special transaction that creates new bitcoin under consensus rules.
Bitcoin’s protocol adjusts mining difficulty to keep block production near an average of about 10 minutes, and the subsidy is reduced by halving events over time.

Ethereum founding and launch timeline.summary: Ethereum was first outlined by Vitalik Buterin in a white paper released in late 2013, proposing a general-purpose blockchain for decentralized applications and smart contracts. Development continued through 2014 and early 2015, including public prototypes and a 2014 ether crowdsale, before the network officially launched as the Frontier release with the genesis block on July 30, 2015.
keyPoints: Vitalik Buterin published the introductory Ethereum white paper in 2013, before the network launched in 2015.
The Ethereum Foundation describes Frontier as the first live release of Ethereum, and its launch announcement states the genesis block was generated on July 30, 2015.
Ethereum’s path to launch included pre-release testing phases such as Olympic in May 2015, which the Foundation called the last development phase before Frontier.
Ethereum.org states the journey from Buterin’s initial 2013 idea to the official network launch took about 20 months.

Smart contract.summary: A smart contract is code and associated state deployed to a blockchain that runs automatically when transactions invoke it and specified conditions are met. Authoritative sources describe smart contracts as self-executing programs whose execution is performed by blockchain nodes, with the resulting state changes recorded on the blockchain.
keyPoints: NIST defines a smart contract as a collection of code and data deployed via cryptographically signed transactions; blockchain nodes execute it, must derive the same result, and record the execution outcome on-chain.
Ethereum documentation says a smart contract is a program that runs at a specific address on the Ethereum blockchain, can define rules, and automatically enforces them through code.
IBM describes smart contracts as digital contracts stored on a blockchain that are automatically executed when predetermined terms and conditions are met, often using "if/when...then..." logic.
Because blockchain execution must be deterministic, smart contracts generally rely on on-chain data unless external information is supplied through mechanisms such as oracles.

Ethereum transition to Proof of Stake vi.canonical: Ethereum transition to Proof of Stake via The Merge
Ethereum transition to Proof of Stake vi.summary: Ethereum’s “Merge” was the network upgrade that combined Ethereum Mainnet with the Beacon Chain and completed Ethereum’s transition from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake on September 15, 2022. After the Merge, Ethereum block production and consensus shifted from miners to validators staking ETH, while the existing chain history, accounts, and smart contracts continued uninterrupted. Official Ethereum sources describe the Merge as a major roadmap milestone that sharply reduced Ethereum’s energy use and established the base for later scaling upgrades.
keyPoints: The Merge was executed on September 15, 2022, when Ethereum Mainnet merged with the Beacon Chain and officially adopted Proof of Stake.
Before the Merge, the Beacon Chain had already been running since December 1, 2020 as Ethereum’s parallel Proof of Stake consensus system.
The Ethereum Foundation’s mainnet announcement said Ethereum would operate entirely under Proof of Stake after the Merge, replacing mining-based Proof of Work consensus.
Ethereum.org states the transition reduced Ethereum’s energy consumption dramatically, commonly cited as about 99.95%, and positioned the network for future roadmap upgrades.

Post-Merge Ethereum energy consumption r.summary: Ethereum’s September 15, 2022 Merge changed Ethereum from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake, eliminating the need for energy-intensive mining. Official Ethereum Foundation and ethereum.org materials consistently state that this cut Ethereum’s energy consumption by about 99.95%, while a newer ethereum.org energy page citing CCRI estimates puts the reduction at more than 99.988% and current annualized electricity use at roughly 0.0026 TWh per year.
keyPoints: The Merge was executed on September 15, 2022, completing Ethereum’s transition to proof-of-stake and retiring proof-of-work mining.
Ethereum Foundation materials published before the event estimated Ethereum would use at least ~99.95% less energy after the Merge.
Current ethereum.org documentation still states the Merge reduced Ethereum’s energy consumption by ~99.95%, and a newer energy-consumption page cites CCRI estimates of a >99.988% drop.
The same ethereum.org energy page reports post-Merge annual electricity consumption of about 2,601 MWh, or 0.0026 TWh per year, for the whole Ethereum network.

Subsequent decline in DeFi TVL.summary: Search results support the concept: DeFi total value locked (TVL) fell sharply after the late-2021/early-2022 peak, with the downturn becoming severe during 2022. CoinGecko’s Q2 2022 report says Ethereum’s TVL was still 52% lower than in Q1 2022, while Terra’s May 2022 collapse wiped out most of that ecosystem’s TVL. Chainalysis also describes TVL as remaining down through 2023 after a sharp mid-2022 decrease, indicating the decline was not just brief volatility but a prolonged contraction in DeFi capital.
keyPoints: CoinGecko reported that in Q2 2022, Ethereum’s overall TVL was still 52% lower than in Q1 2022, even as Ethereum regained share versus shrinking alt-chain ecosystems.
Terra’s collapse was a major driver of the decline: CoinGecko says Terra’s TVL fell to about 5% of multichain share, with just over $21 million left on the network by end-June 2022.
Chainalysis states DeFi TVL was down throughout 2023 following a sharp decrease in the middle of 2022, showing the post-2021 decline persisted well beyond the initial crash.

Later market reports show TVL eventually recovered somewhat but stayed far below the 2021-era boom for many ecosystems; for example, CoinGecko noted Avalanche was down from a peak of over $10 billion TVL in Q4 2021 to about $890 million by September 2024.

DeFi TVL peak in late 2021.summary: Search results support the concept that DeFi total value locked reached its cycle high in late 2021, but the exact figure varies by data source and methodology. Cointelegraph, citing DefiLlama, says DeFi TVL peaked at about $180 billion in November 2021, while The Block reported DeFiLlama showing the sector exceeding $200 billion in October 2021, and CoinGecko’s 2021 year-end report put the November 2021 all-time high at $174 billion.
keyPoints: Cointelegraph reported that DeFi TVL fell 80% from a November 2021 peak of $180 billion to about $37 billion by October 2023, explicitly identifying November 2021 as the peak month.

The Block reported on October 13, 2021 that DefiLlama showed DeFi TVL had exceeded $200 billion across multiple blockchains, indicating some trackers recorded a peak above $180 billion even before year-end.
CoinGecko’s 2021 Year End Cryptocurrency Report stated DeFi reached an all-time high of $174 billion in November 2021, showing that different providers produced slightly different peak estimates.

The variation between roughly $174 billion, $180 billion, and $200 billion likely reflects differences in coverage and counting methodology across trackers, especially across multiple chains and possible double-counting of rehypothecated assets.

sources.title: DeFi is ‘waking up again’ — Active loans return to 2022 levels
Decentralized finance is now a $200 billion industry | The Block
CoinGecko 2021 Year End Cryptocurrency Report

Total value locked (TVL).summary: Total value locked (TVL) is a DeFi metric that measures the total market value of cryptoassets deposited in a protocol’s smart contracts and actively used for functions such as lending, borrowing, liquidity provision, asset management, or insurance. It is usually expressed in U.S. dollars and is widely used as a proxy for protocol scale, liquidity, and user confidence, though it can fluctuate with both deposits/withdrawals and changes in token prices.
keyPoints: DeFi Pulse defines TVL as the total value of assets deposited in a DeFi protocol that are generating economic activity, and excludes assets minted by the protocol itself, illiquid assets, and assets not actively used in the protocol.

CoinMarketCap explains that TVL is typically calculated as the sum of each locked token amount multiplied by its current market price, so TVL can change even when user balances stay constant if token prices move.

TVL is commonly interpreted as an indicator of trust, adoption, and liquidity: higher TVL generally suggests more capital committed to a protocol and better capacity to support trading, lending, or other DeFi activity.

Chainlink notes a limitation of TVL-based evaluation: protocols can become overly optimized for growing TVL, so TVL should be used alongside other metrics rather than as a complete measure of product-market fit or real usage.

Uniswap pioneered the automated market m.canonical: Uniswap pioneered the automated market maker model
Uniswap pioneered the automated market m.summary: Search results support the claim with an important nuance: Uniswap did not invent automated market makers in the broadest theoretical sense, but it is widely credited with pioneering and popularizing the AMM model for decentralized exchanges by launching Uniswap v1 in 2018 and introducing the constant-product design (x*y=k) that became the template for many later DEXs.
keyPoints: Uniswap’s own documentation states that “the Uniswap Protocol pioneered this AMM model,” describing AMMs as smart-contract-based exchanges that replace order books with liquidity pools.
Academic and technical sources trace the constant-product AMM used by Uniswap to Hayden Adams in 2017, with Uniswap v1 launching in 2018 as the original proof of concept on Ethereum.
Scholarly reviews describe Uniswap v1/v2 as the canonical constant-product market maker (CPMM), using the formula x*y=k to price trades algorithmically.
Multiple later analyses say Uniswap popularized or established the liquidity-pool/AMM approach for DeFi, even though related market-making ideas and alternative mechanisms existed before it.

Uniswap trading volume since launch.summary: The claim that Uniswap processed over $1.8 trillion in trading volume since launch is outdated. Search results show Uniswap crossed $1 trillion in lifetime volume in May 2022, exceeded $2 trillion by late 2023, and reached about $3 trillion in all-time volume by mid-2025, making it the first DEX reported to hit that milestone.
keyPoints: Uniswap launched in November 2018 and surpassed $1 trillion in cumulative trading volume in May 2022, according to coverage citing Uniswap and market data.
By late 2023, reports said Uniswap had exceeded $2 trillion in all-time trading volume, indicating that "$1.8 trillion since launch" was only accurate before that milestone.
By mid-2025, multiple reports stated Uniswap had reached roughly $3 trillion in cumulative trading volume, the first decentralized exchange to do so.

Third-party 2025 recap posts and analytics pages suggest cumulative volume moved beyond $4 trillion by the end of 2025, but these later figures are less authoritative than the earlier milestone reporting.

Automated market maker model.summary: An automated market maker (AMM) is a decentralized exchange model that replaces order books with smart-contract liquidity pools. In the common constant-product form popularized by Uniswap v2, liquidity providers deposit two tokens of equal market value into a pool, traders swap against that pool, and the token ratio changes to determine price according to a formula such as x*y=k.
keyPoints: Liquidity providers (LPs) typically supply a token pair in equal value terms; in return, they receive LP tokens or an accounting claim representing their proportional share of the pool and its fees.
In constant-product AMMs, the pool keeps the product of reserves approximately constant (x*y=k), so larger trades move the price more and create slippage when pool depth is limited.
AMM trades execute directly against pooled reserves in smart contracts rather than matching buyers and sellers in an order book.

Later AMM designs extended the basic two-token-pair model: for example, Uniswap documentation notes that v2 uses full-range liquidity for token pairs, while newer designs such as concentrated liquidity change how capital is allocated across prices.

SEC classification of cryptocurrencies a.canonical: SEC classification of cryptocurrencies as securities with Bitcoin and Ethereum exceptions
SEC classification of cryptocurrencies a.summary: SEC materials support the general concept only in a qualified sense: the agency has repeatedly said many crypto assets may be securities under the federal securities laws, applying the Howey investment-contract test case by case, but it has not adopted a formal rule declaring "most cryptocurrencies" to be securities. SEC officials have publicly indicated that Bitcoin is not viewed as a security in its current form, and a 2018 SEC speech said Ether was not a security based on the Ethereum network’s then-current decentralization; however, SEC filings also emphasize those statements were non-binding staff or official views rather than definitive Commission-wide rules.
keyPoints: In 2018, SEC Chairman Jay Clayton testified that some cryptocurrencies "do not appear to be securities," but labels like "currency" do not control; the analysis depends on facts and whether investors expect profits from others’ efforts.

William Hinman, then Director of Corporation Finance, said in a June 14, 2018 SEC speech that offers and sales of Ether were not securities transactions at that time because the Ethereum network had become sufficiently decentralized.

Multiple SEC-filed disclosures later summarized the agency view as follows: Bitcoin and Ether were the only crypto assets senior SEC officials had publicly said were unlikely to be securities, while other assets lacked that same public treatment.

Recent SEC correspondence in February 2025 pushed back on issuer disclosures characterizing Ether as clearly outside securities status, underscoring that earlier public statements were not binding SEC policy and that the Commission has identified numerous crypto assets as securities in enforcement matters.

Programmatic token sales on exchanges no.canonical: Programmatic token sales on exchanges not necessarily securities transactions
Programmatic token sales on exchanges no.summary: In SEC v. Ripple Labs, Judge Analisa Torres held in July 2023 that Ripple’s "programmatic sales" of XRP on digital asset exchanges did not, on the specific facts before the court, constitute offers or sales of investment contracts under Howey. The ruling turned on transaction context rather than declaring XRP itself a security or non-security: institutional sales made directly to sophisticated buyers were held to be securities transactions, while exchange-based sales executed through blind bid/ask mechanisms were not. Subsequent filings and market commentary have treated the decision as support for the narrower proposition that some anonymous, exchange-based token sales may fall outside securities laws, but the opinion is fact-specific and not a blanket rule for all token sales.
keyPoints: The July 13, 2023 summary judgment decision distinguished among Ripple’s institutional sales, programmatic exchange sales, and other distributions; only the institutional sales were held to be unregistered securities transactions.

For programmatic sales, the court emphasized that exchange buyers typically did not know whether they were buying from Ripple or another seller, weakening the expectation-of-profits-from-Ripple showing under Howey.

Multiple legal analyses note the decision was transaction-specific: it did not hold that XRP itself is inherently not a security, but that certain sales mechanisms on exchanges were not investment contracts on those facts.

Later SEC-related and market filings continued to describe Ripple as standing for the proposition that programmatic and some secondary-market XRP sales were not treated as securities transactions, while the case itself continued through remedies and appellate activity after the 2023 ruling.

sources.title: Ripple Labs: District Court Holds That Direct Digital Token Sales Constituted Investment Contracts Under Howey, But Other XRP Distributions Did Not
Changing Tides or a Ripple in Still Water? Examining the SEC v. Ripple Ruling
SEC v. Ripple Labs: Securities Law Analysis Under Howey Applied on a Transaction-by-Transaction Basis
SEC filing discussing XRP treatment in light of Ripple decision
SEC filing discussing Ripple programmatic trading on secondary markets

SEC v. Ripple.summary: In SEC v. Ripple Labs, Judge Analisa Torres’s July 13, 2023 summary-judgment ruling drew a transaction-specific distinction under Howey: Ripple’s direct institutional XRP sales were held to be unregistered investment contracts, while its "programmatic sales" on digital-asset exchanges were not, because those exchange trades were blind bid/ask transactions and buyers generally could not know whether they were purchasing from Ripple or link their expected profits specifically to Ripple’s efforts. The ruling did not hold that XRP itself is inherently a security; instead, it analyzed the circumstances of particular sales and distributions.
keyPoints: The Southern District of New York held that XRP "is not in and of itself" a security; the legal analysis turned on the contract, transaction, or scheme surrounding each category of sale.

For programmatic sales on exchanges, the court found purchasers were in blind bid/ask transactions and usually did not know if their money was going to Ripple, which undercut the SEC’s theory that those trades satisfied Howey’s expectation-of-profits-from-the-efforts-of-others prong.
The court reached the opposite result for institutional sales, finding Ripple’s direct sales to sophisticated counterparties under written contracts were unregistered securities transactions.

Later SEC materials and market disclosures continued to describe the case as a mixed ruling: institutional sales violated Section 5, while exchange-based/programmatic sales and certain other distributions did not.

sources.title: SEC.gov | Statement on the Agency’s Settlement with Ripple Labs, Inc.
